title: SQL Data Management
subtitle: Add, change, remove, and structure relational data with deliberate safety checks.
description: A focused SQLite course with three standard modules and one final capstone. Learners move from read-only relational reporting into safe mutations and table creation without repeating SELECT, filtering, reporting, or join lessons.
audience: Learners who completed SQL Foundations, SQL Analysis & Reporting, and Multi-Table SQL & Joins.
Beginners who can already identify rows safely with WHERE and understand primary-key and foreign-key relationships.
Learners preparing to create and maintain small relational datasets before studying database design.
prerequisites: Write reliable SELECT and WHERE queries that identify exact target rows.
Use NULL checks, aliases, sorting, and deterministic verification queries.
Read primary-key and foreign-key relationships in a schema.
Work confidently in the browser SQL editor and results table.
goals: Use a preview-change-verify-reset workflow for data-changing SQL.
Insert rows with explicit columns, intentional NULL values, and controlled defaults.
Update and delete only confirmed rows with required WHERE clauses.
Create tables with keys, required values, uniqueness, defaults, and foreign keys.
Complete three cumulative module projects and one integrated data-management capstone.

preferredTerms.preview: run a SELECT with the intended WHERE condition
preferredTerms.runWorkspace: click Run query to execute the SQL files in their authored order

notes: This course has four modules total: three standard modules plus one final capstone. Do not split INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or individual constraints into repetitive extra modules.
Assume learners already know SELECT, WHERE, NULL checks, aliases, sorting, aggregates, GROUP BY, and joins. Use those as tools for preview and verification; never create standalone review topics.
Every technical lesson has exactly two distinct sketches and two aligned code_input Try It exercises.
Worked examples, Try It tasks, module projects, and capstone steps must use different records, values, tables, and expected final states.
Modules 0 and 1 use inventory_ops. Modules 2 and 3 use ddl_blank.

Every INSERT must use an explicit column list. To use a column default in SQLite, omit that column from the explicit list; do not place DEFAULT inside a VALUES tuple. Every UPDATE and DELETE solution must include WHERE.
An unsafe no-WHERE mutation may appear only as clearly labeled non-executable diagnosis prose; it must never be a solutionCode or runnable starter.
Every mutation and DDL exercise must include result-returning checkSql that verifies the final state.
Each standard module has exactly one progressive module project. Only the final topic is rendered as a capstone.
A progressive data-management step is a complete cumulative SQL workspace. Step N+1 starterFiles must carry forward step N solutionFiles, and starterCode/solutionCode represent the entry file only.

CREATE TABLE work starts from ddl_blank. Module 2 uses schema.sql plus query.sql; the capstone uses schema.sql, seed.sql, and query.sql. Later steps replace complete files rather than using ALTER TABLE or DROP TABLE.
Do not introduce transactions, UPSERT, REPLACE, indexes, views, triggers, stored procedures, subqueries, CTEs, set operations, or window functions.

SQL file architecture is progressive: Modules 0-1 use one query.sql entry file; Module 2 uses schema.sql then query.sql; Module 3 uses schema.sql, seed.sql, then query.sql. The compiler executes files in sqlFileOrder and opens query.sql as the entry file.
Use the Tables tab for schema reading. ERD and Chen remain hidden in this course because database design is taught next.
Use exactly one authored SQL file named query.sql.
Set entryFilePath to query.sql. Keep starterFiles and solutionFiles limited to that one file when they are present.
SELECT previews and verification queries remain in query.sql with INSERT statements.
Use exactly one authored SQL file named query.sql.
Set entryFilePath to query.sql. Keep starterFiles and solutionFiles limited to that one file when they are present.
Preview, UPDATE or DELETE, and verification SELECT statements stay together in query.sql.
Use exactly two learner-editable files: schema.sql and query.sql.
Set entryFilePath to query.sql and sqlFileOrder to ["schema.sql", "query.sql"].
schema.sql contains complete CREATE TABLE definitions. query.sql contains schema-inspection SELECT statements.
Every starterFiles and solutionFiles array must include both files with query.sql marked as the entry file.
Use exactly three learner-editable files: schema.sql, seed.sql, and query.sql.
Set entryFilePath to query.sql and sqlFileOrder to ["schema.sql", "seed.sql", "query.sql"].
schema.sql owns CREATE TABLE statements, seed.sql owns approved INSERT statements, and query.sql owns UPDATE, DELETE, and final verification SELECT statements.
Every starterFiles and solutionFiles array must include all three files with query.sql marked as the entry file.
